frameworks.name: sensorimotor transformation
frameworks.one_line: Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex converts visual cue location into saccade direction during delay.

frameworks.name: spatial working memory
frameworks.one_line: Delay-period activity holds spatial information across a memory interval.

core_question: Does prefrontal task-related activity during spatial working memory encode the visual cue location or the upcoming saccade direction?
key_assumptions: Single-neuron directional tuning can be dissociated by rotating the cue-to-saccade mapping
Cue and saccade direction can be decoupled by a 90-degree rotation rule
manipulations: Task rule: standard ODR vs 90-degree clockwise rotatory ODR
Task epoch: cue, delay, response periods
key_findings: Cue-period directional activity predominantly encoded visual cue location
Most delay-period directional activity encoded cue location; a minority encoded saccade direction
Response-period activity more often encoded saccade direction, with a substantial fraction postsaccadic
Population delay activity for cue location was tonic, while saccade-direction population activity ramped up gradually
interpretations: Visual-to-motor transformation occurs gradually within dorsolateral prefrontal cortex across the delay
Postsaccadic cue-location activity may serve to terminate no-longer-needed delay activity
